evidence_quotes.quote: video games in their digital format: whether they are singleplayers, or multi-player.
license to use a game, and that particular title is intended to ‘expire’ and no longer be available beyond a certain date
reserves the right to remove access to the content purchased
without any reimbursement or anything else

paragraphs.text: Thanks for this opportunity to be able to talk about how the European Community will take shape, including on aspects such as the focus on the relationship between customer and digital provider of any good. Like many others, I would like to draw attention to video games in their digital format: whether they are singleplayers, or multi-player. I believe that like all other forms of entertainment, video games deserve to be preserved, as we already do with films, music, books and works of art in general. However, apart from this more general discourse, there is a lot of confusion, at present, about the dynamics of digital licences, sold through platforms such as: STEAM, Origins, Epic Store,... If I, user, purchase via a digital medium, a licence to use a game, and that particular title is intended to ‘expire’ and no longer be available beyond a certain date, it would be right to know it very clearly at the time of purchase, at least to help me make my decision. So far, I have always believed that the section often used in all ‘HRT’ for digital products, where it is specified that the distributor, or who for him, reserves the right to remove access to the content purchased, was addressed to whom that content was abusing, through the practice of ‘cheating’ online, or to obtain some illegal gain from it. But this seems not only to be the case. I am talking about personal experience, as the owner of the game ‘The Crew’, purchased through the official Ubisoft store, and currently inaccessible, also to be able to play singleplayer, a title which I bought with my money and which Ubisoft considered it absolutely correct to remove me from the bookshop, without any reimbursement or anything else, if not just a warning that would have been removed at the end of 30 days without me being able to do anything to keep it: how to have an executable to turn a server locally, on my computer, absolutely at no extra cost or additional liability on the part of Ubisoft, for example. At present, digital stores are beginning to be no longer an optional choice and increasingly the practice is developing to release titles only in their digital version, without printing, as in the past, an analogue medium that is a CD, DVD or Bluray. In the future, digital will probably be the only practice. As for me, it is possible to repull an old 90/2000 console and play in any title in my possession, without any online and offline support from developers and/or distributors, so I think it should be the standard for digital services. Once I buy a good, spend my money and support the industry behind gaming, and above all do not abuse the product in any unlawful way, I think it is my right to enjoy that good for as long as possible, or at least without the possibility for the industry itself, which with my money continues to work, to remove my access to content, simply by pressing a button (metaphorically speaking) and wasting the hands of any responsibility. Thank you for your attention and good work.
